title: Facile and cost-effective non-HPLC purification of Gallium-68 labeled peptides after in-house DOTAGA and NOTA conjugation
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"x20c5","text":"The positron emission tomography (PET) facilitates the functional imaging of the clinical ailment by in-vivo administration of a molecule tagged with a positron-emitting radioisotope. To facilitate covalent linkage between the molecule and positron-emitting Gallium-68 (Ga-68), the bifunctional chelating agents (BFC) are used. An essential step after conjugation of a peptide is the removal of the leftover unconjugated BFC. Generally, this is accomplished using HPLC, worldwide. However, the non-availability of HPLC in hospital radiopharmacy is a limiting factor hampering the basic small-scale research. Therefore, a facile and easily available purification method for conjugated small peptides is the need of the hour. It may support and expand the small-scale research even within the limited resources. This is an attempt to explore the use of classical hydrophobic interaction-based purification in the field of the development of novel radiopharmaceuticals. Removal of unconjugated chelating agents in both radiolabeled and non-radiolabeled forms can be accomplished using Sep-PAK-based cartridges. The Sep-PAK cartridges are routinely used for Ga-68 based radiopharmaceuticals to get rid of unlabeled Ga-68. Using a similar cartridge, the unconjugated chelating agent can also be removed by adding a single washing step with a low ethanol concentration. The whole process of purification to get rid of Ga-68 and Ga-bifunctional chelating agents can be accomplished in less than 5 min using a C-18 cartridge.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"neogl","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"kvrqd","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"kennv","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"i7ezk","text":"The field of nuclear medicine enables functional imaging and treatment of various malignancies by targeting molecular changes such as overexpression of receptors on tumor tissue. Small peptide molecules are often used as ligands for these overexpressed receptors to help in managing the tumors. These specific peptides when tagged with a radiometal, facilitate non-invasive imaging using SPECT or PET cameras and may provide information on the type of tumor and spread of the disease. As these peptides are specific to receptors expressed on tumors, radiolabeling of peptides with radionuclide of interest generally requires the incorporation of bifunctional chelator (BFC) to minimize the change in the conformational changes that may, in turn, affect the biological activity of the peptide. The BFC possesses a functional linker group and electron-donating moieties. The functional linker groups such as the isothiocyanate or amide group enable conjugation with amine group of the peptide and the strong electron-donating moieties (O, F, N) facilitate chelation with the radio-metal. To ensure effective conjugation yield, a higher molar ratio of BFC to the peptide is often required. Therefore, in the final reaction mixture besides the desired conjugated peptide, the unconjugated BFC is also present1-2. The presence of unconjugated BFC subsequently reduces the radiochemical purity and may mislead the imaging outcome. Routinely, the excess leftover unconjugated BFC is removed using HPLC and pure conjugated peptide is lyophilized for further use. However, in the hospital radiopharmacy setting, the availability of HPLC is a limiting factor that majorly affects the potent small-scale research. Further lyophilization of conjugated molecules in an inert atmosphere adds to the cost. Consequently, the custom synthesized conjugated peptides are purchased at high prices. Therefore, the development of a simple and cost-effective procedure to achieve the purified conjugated or radiolabeled product is the need of the hour. In the current study, a purification method for the separation of unconjugated chelating agents from the in-house conjugated small peptide molecules has been successfully attempted.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":1304,"length":3}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"tdixw","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

Add 10 mL of HPLC water followed by vigorous shaking upto clear solution formation. Confirm the solution pH at 7.0 using pH paper and pass from 0.22 μm filters. The buffer can be stored in dark at room temperature.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":38}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"h8sis","text":"Sodium Acetate Buffer (0.25 M, pH 4): Weigh 1.025 g sodium acetate powder in a plastic tube and add 50 mL water to it. The buffer can be stored in dark at room temperature.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":37}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"acwwj","text":"Sodium carbonate (0.1 M, pH 9): Weigh 0.529 g sodium carbonate powder and 0.420 g sodium hydrogen carbonate separately in sterile plastic tubes of 50 mL. For final preparation of 0.1M sodium carbonate buffer of pH 9, mix 2 mL of sodium carbonate with 8mL of sodium bicarbonate. Mix the solution well and pass from 0.22 μm filters. The buffer can be stored in dark at room temperature.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":32}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"zie4r","text":"Sodium Citrate (0.5 M, pH 3.5): Weigh 26.8 mg di-sodium hydrogen citrate-1,5 hydrate and 153.7 mg tri-sodium citrate dehydrate in a sterile plastic tube and dissolve in 5.3 mL HPLC water. Add 1.3 mL of 2M HCl (1.67 mL HCl to 10 mL HPLC water) to adjust the pH at 2.5. steps: Conjugation of different peptides is performed in four different reaction conditions, separately with BFCs mentioned below in detail.
Calculate the amount of p-SCN-Bn-NOTA required to provide two-fold molar excess to the amount of peptide.
Weigh a minimum of 1 mg peptide in a plastic microtube. ! CAUTION Use of any metallic equipment should be completely avoided throughout the process as it can hinder radiolabeling.
Dissolve the peptide in a 10 μL volume of DMF and vortex for 1 min. ! CAUTION Volume of DMF should not be increased 0.2% v/v of final reaction.

Slowly add 80 - 100 μL of 0.1 M phosphate buffer to the peptide mixture, pH 7 followed by slow addition of 0.1 M sodium carbonate buffer to attain pH 9 (typically 50 – 70 μL). ! CAUTION Addition of phosphate buffer restrains the sudden shift of pH from acidic to basic which otherwise sometimes leads to precipitate formation.

Separately in another plastic microtube, dissolve the calculated amount of p-SCN-Bn-NOTA in 10 μL DMF and add 40 μL 0.1 M sodium bicarbonate buffer to attain pH 9. ! CAUTION Dissolve the chelating agents in weak acids to avoid hydrolysis.

Transfer the resulting p-SCN-Bn-NOTA volume to peptide mixture (Step 4) and vortex for 1 min. ! CAUTION Keep the reaction volume as minimum as possible for high conjugation yield. Peptide and chelating agents should be freshly dissolved. ! PAUSE POINT Incubate the conjugation reaction mixture at 4ºC for a period of 72 h ensuring the final reaction pH 9. ! CAUTION Conjugation yield increases with an increase in incubation time.
The protocol for conjugation of the peptide with p-SCN-Bn-DOTA-GA is the same as that with p-SCN-Bn-NOTA with the following changes:
p-SCN-Bn-DOTA-GA can be dissolved in DMSO as well whereas p-SCN-Bn-NOTA gets precipitated in DMSO.
Requirement of the peptide to p-SCN-Bn-DOTA-GA molar ratio is relatively higher.
Requires approximately a relatively high volume of sodium bicarbonate buffer (100 -110 μL) for attaining pH 9 for the chelating agent mixture.
! CAUTION The experimental part involving radioactivity should be performed by a certified person as per the guidelines of local radiation safety norms.
Elute 68 Ga from 68 Ge- 68 Ga generator in a sterile glass vial using 4 mL 0.04M HCl as 68 GaCl 3 .

Add 820-840 μL of 0.25 M sodium acetate buffer to attain pH 4. ! CRITICAL STEP The reaction pH should be strictly maintained at 4 for p-SCN-Bn-NOTA conjugated peptides and 4.5 for p-SCN-Bn-DOTAGA conjugated peptides.

From the crude conjugation reaction mixture, pipette the volume required so as to obtain 15μg of peptide (non-purified containing both conjugated and unconjugated peptide) and transfer to 68 GaCl 3 solution. ! CAUTION the amount of radioactivity typically ranges from 15-20 mCi for 15 μg of the non-purified conjugated peptide.
Incubate the radiolabeling reaction mixture at 95ºC for 10 min.

Pass 5 mL of 70% ethanol through a Sep-Pak C18 Plus light cartridge followed by 10 mL HPLC water in order to activate the cartridge. ! CRITICAL STEP Cartridge conditioning for wetting the silica bed is required in order to increase the surface area available trapping the analyte. The cartridge conditioning should be performed before starting the radiolabeling procedure to avoid radioactivity decay of Ga-68.

Allow trapping of radiolabeled analytes by slowly passing the radiolabeled mixture through the cartridge. The radiolabeled conjugated peptide and radiolabeled unconjugated chelating agent both get trapped in the cartridge while the free Ga-68 passes through the cartridge.

Slowly pass 10 mL of 15% ethanol through the cartridge in order to elute the radiolabeled unconjugated chelating agent and free Ga-68 if present. ! CRITICAL STEP Concentration of ethanol can vary from 12-15 % for eluting the radiolabeled BFC attributing to the variability in hydrophobic strength of peptide molecules analyzed.
In order to wash the cartridge, pass 2 mL of normal saline from the cartridge.
Elute the final product with 1 mL 50% ethanol in a sterile glass vial.
Transfer 0.5 M sodium citrate buffer in a 12 cm tall glass test tube until the 0.5 cm mark.
Place a 3 μL spot of the analyte at a 1 cm pencil mark of Whatman 3MM and TLC Silica gel strips.

Allow it to dry. Gently place the strips using a forcep in glass tubes with sodium citrate buffer and allow the solvent to run up to the mark of 9 cm. ! CAUTION Strips should be precisely placed in solvent without dipping the drop inside the mobile phase.
Remove the strip using a forcep and cover the dried strips with plastic tape in order to avoid radioactivity contamination on the Radio-TLC scanner plate.
Set the scanning time of up to 1 min to obtain the chromatogram and calculate the retention time of different radiolabeled components using software such as GINA Software.
Calculate retention factor for different possible radiolabeled components present in mixture using the formula: Rf= Distance traveled by solvent front / Distance traveled by the solute front
The retention factor values of different possible radio-chemicals are:
Free unconjugated 68 Ga- p-SCN-Bn-DOTAGA or 68 Ga- p-SCN-Bn-NOTA has R f value 1.00 using Whatman 3MM and sodium citrate as stationary and mobile phase, respectively.
Free Ga-68 has an R f value of 1.00 using TLC Silica gel strips and sodium citrate as stationary and mobile phases, respectively.
Most of the 68 Ga- p-SCN-Bn-DOTAGA-Peptide or 68 Ga- p-SCN-Bn-NOTA-Peptide has R f value 0.00 using both TLC Silica gel strips and Whatman 3MM as stationary phase and sodium citrate as mobile phase.
In absence of a Radio-TLC scanner simply cut the dried strips in two halves as the upper half of solvent front and the lower half of solute front.

Measure counts in well counter depending concentration of radioactivity and calculate % unconjugated chelating agent in the Whatman 3MM/sodium citrate and free Ga-68 in Whatman 3MM /TLC Silica gel strips combination as follows: % unconjugated BFC or Ga-68=(Counts in the upper half of strip)/(Counts in both lower and upper half)×100

Similarly calculate % bound peptide as follows: % bound peptide =(Counts in the lower half of strip)/(Counts in both lower and upper half)×100 In order to determine the conjugation yield, Radio-TLC chromatography of crude radiolabeled reaction mixture obtained before purification should be performed.

In step 16, fine-tuning for the concentration of ethanol is required to avoid elution of conjugated peptides along with the BFC. The concentration of ethanol can vary from 12-15% depending on variability in hydrophobic strength of peptide and chelating agent with the C18 cartridge.
1. Conjugation: ~ 12 h - 3 days (Can vary from peptide to peptide) 2. Radiolabeling Timing ~ 10 min 3. Purification Timing ~ 5 min

The technique offers a simple, facile, and cost-effective practical approach for the purification of in-house conjugated new peptide molecules. The peptide molecule can be purified either just after conjugation with BFCs or after radiolabeling with Ga-68 with provided radiochemical purity of >99.9%. This technique may globally enhance the small-scale radiopharmacy research to explore the theranostic potential of new peptides.
